title: Fast Tensor Network Imaginary Time Evolution by Implicit Stepping on Logarithmic Grids
authors: John P. Zima, E. Miles Stoudenmire, Steven R. White, Olivier Parcollet, Jason Kaye
abstract: We present a new method for the efficient imaginary time evolution of quantum many-body wavefunctions represented by matrix product states (MPS). We first show that logarithmic time grids are sufficient to resolve long imaginary time dynamics, yielding an exponential reduction in the number of time steps compared with standard approaches. We then show that A-stable implicit time-stepping methods for ordinary differential equations allow stable propagation for any time step size. The resulting scheme requires only matrix-vector products and linear solves, standard operations in the MPS toolbox. We validate our approach with two examples: a Heisenberg spin chain, which we use to demonstrate a speedup of several orders of magnitude over the standard time-dependent variational principle method with uniform time steps, and a single-site Anderson impurity model with a metallic bath, for which propagation to large imaginary times allows one to observe the exponential dependence of the Kondo temperature on the interaction strength.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, highly efficient technique for simulating imaginary time evolution of quantum many-body systems using Tensor Networks. By combining logarithmic time grids with A-stable implicit solvers, the method achieves a dramatic reduction in computational cost, scaling logarithmically with the required evolution time. This breakthrough allows for accurate simulations of complex models like the Anderson impurity model to very low temperatures.
main_problem: The primary goal is to achieve an efficient and stable method for performing imaginary time evolution of quantum many-body wavefunctions represented by Matrix Product States (MPS).
main_result: The combination of using a logarithmic time grid and A-stable implicit time-stepping methods allows for an exponential reduction in the required number of time steps compared to standard uniform-step approaches.
method: The authors employ A-stable implicit time-stepping methods (like the trapezoid rule) applied to the imaginary time evolution ODE, utilizing a logarithmic time grid to resolve long-time dynamics efficiently.
model_or_system: The method is tested on the Heisenberg spin chain and the single-site Anderson impurity model with a metallic bath, which are canonical models in strongly correlated electron physics.
key_observables: Overlap error, Kondo temperature dependence, and the double occupancy $D(T)$ in the Anderson model.
important_parameters: The maximum imaginary time $	au_{\max}$ (or inverse temperature $ eta$), and the bond dimension $\chi$.
main_assumptions: The method assumes the Hamiltonian is positive semi-definite and relies on the stability properties of A-stable implicit solvers, which overcome the stability constraints of explicit methods.
figures_summary: Figures compare CPU time and overlap error showing logarithmic scaling for advanced methods on the Heisenberg chain, and demonstrate the collapse of $D(T)/D(0)$ vs. $T/T_K(U)$ for the Anderson model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem, details the logarithmic grid and implicit solver combination, validates the approach on the Heisenberg chain (showing speedup), and applies it to the Anderson impurity model to observe physical phenomena like the Kondo temperature.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, scalable numerical tool for simulating thermal and ground-state properties of strongly correlated materials, directly impacting research in high-Tc superconductivity and quantum impurity physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Imaginary time evolution, starting from a N´eel state, for a 100-spin Heisenberg chain. Top panel: CPU time vs. τ for uniform-stepping TDVP with a time step of 0.1, trapezoid method with n = 4 time steps per panel, and spectral method with comparable accuracy. Upper inset: bond dimensions of methods shown (taking max over all bonds), fixing the dis- carded weight (i.e., total discarded squared Schmidt weight at a bond) to 10−10. Lower inset: zoom of CPU times. The gray lines are linear fits of the last decade in τ, showing loga- rithmic scaling with respect to τ of the trapezoid and spectral methods once the bond dimension saturates. FIG. 3. (Top) Double occupancy D(T) of the Anderson im- purity model vs. T/TK(U). TK(U) are adjusted to obtain a collapse of the curves for various U. Error bars are 95% confi- dence intervals of METTS sampling error. D(0) is measured directly in the ground state. (Inset) TK vs. the interaction strength U, showing an exponential dependence on U. The dashed curve is a fit TK(U) ≈0.057 × e−0.21U. (Bottom) Average CPU time of the computation of individual METTS versus temperature for different U, showing a log β scaling. The straight dashed line is a linear fit.
